title: Using wood ash in the garden and compost
subtitle: How to apply ash from wood fires safely without disrupting soil pH
excerpt: Wood ash from open fires and wood stoves is alkaline and contains potassium and calcium. It can be a useful soil amendment but raises pH: too much disrupts plant nutrition. This guide covers safe quantities, application methods, and what never to add.
sourceNotes: Royal Horticultural Society guidance on wood ash as a soil amendment. Garden Organic composting materials factsheet. NIAB soil pH adjustment guidance for UK vegetable and fruit growing.

content.text: Fresh wood ash has a pH of 9 to 11 and contains approximately 5 to 10% potassium (as potassium carbonate), 30 to 40% calcium (as calcium carbonate), and trace amounts of phosphorus, magnesium, and micronutrients. Rain dissolves and leaches the soluble potassium quickly: ash stored in the open for more than a week loses most of its potassium content. Store ash dry until use.

attrs.body: Use only ash from untreated, unpainted wood. Coal ash and ash from treated or painted wood contains harmful residues and must not be composted.
attrs.title: Coal ash is not the same

content.text: A vegetable garden bed that has not received lime or ash recently can take approximately 100 to 150 g per m² of wood ash per year without a problematic pH rise. Most UK soils are naturally slightly acidic (pH 6.0 to 6.5). A single light dressing of ash raises pH by approximately 0.1 to 0.2 units. Repeated applications without a soil pH test raise the risk of over-liming, which locks up phosphorus, manganese, and iron, causing yellowing foliage.

Brassica beds: cabbages, kale, broccoli, and Brussels sprouts prefer a slightly alkaline soil (pH 7.0 to 7.5) and benefit from periodic ash application. It also raises pH above the threshold where clubroot spores are active.
Fruit beds: potassium improves fruit quality in strawberries, raspberries, and gooseberries. Apply around the plants in winter, not in contact with emerging growth.
Lawns: a light dusting reduces moss on acidic lawns. Apply in dry weather to avoid creating a caustic paste on the grass surface.
Potato beds: high pH encourages common scab (Streptomyces scabies). Keep ash away from potato ground.
Acid-loving plants: blueberries, rhododendrons, camellias, and heathers require pH 4.5 to 5.5. Any ash application will harm them.
Onion family: leeks and onions are sensitive to high pH and to potassium excess.
Adding ash to the compost bin

Small amounts of wood ash (a thin scattering, not a concentrated layer) can be added to a compost bin to neutralise acidity from large quantities of fruit and vegetable scraps. The key word is small: concentrated layers of ash raise the pH so high that the bacteria responsible for composting are inhibited. A thin scattering over the top of a new layer of kitchen waste is the correct approach. The
of the heap is more reliably maintained with cardboard and straw than with ash.
of ash to the finished compost is minor. Apply ash directly to beds rather than routing it through the compost bin if soil pH adjustment is the goal.
